description:
## Problem Statement

Beadsmith currently decides its Current Workspace from the desktop process working directory. That was useful during early development, but it makes the directory from which the app was launched an ambient, process-global input rather than a user choice. A user cannot reliably choose another local Beadwork repository, return to a previously used repository, or switch between repositories while Beadsmith remains open.

This is more than a setup inconvenience. During a switch, a premature UI update or an in-flight command using the wrong working directory could present Issues from one Workspace under the name of another. A failed switch could also discard a known-good Issue Explorer snapshot. The app needs a deliberate Workspace lifecycle that makes selection, persistence, validation, loading, and failure recovery understandable and safe.

## Solution

Beadsmith will provide a compact inline Current Workspace selector in the existing sidebar. Users can choose a local directory with the native operating-system picker, select a remembered Workspace, retry an unavailable Workspace, or remove a Workspace from Beadsmith local memory.

A Workspace is the Git repository root initialized for Beadwork. Beadsmith validates a candidate before it can become known, normalizes a selected subdirectory to its repository root, and remembers validated roots in a persistent MRU Workspace Catalog. The backend owns the catalog, Pending Workspace, and optional Current Workspace. It loads Beadwork Issue Explorer data using an explicit working directory for every command; frontend issue-loading operations never choose a path.

Switching is atomic from the user perspective. The existing Current Workspace and its complete Issue Explorer snapshot remain visible while a candidate validates and loads. The candidate becomes Current only after All Issues, Ready, and Blocked load successfully and the new Current Workspace plus MRU update are durably saved. Invalid selections produce inline feedback. Post-validation load or persistence failures preserve the old snapshot, use a dismissible retryable banner, and retain the valid candidate for retry.

## User Stories

1. As a first-time Beadsmith user, I want the app to show an empty workspace chooser rather than infer a repository from its launch directory, so that I explicitly control what data Beadsmith displays.
2. As a user, I want to open the native directory picker from the Current Workspace control, so that I can choose a repository without typing a filesystem path.
3. As a user, I want cancelling the directory picker to leave the app unchanged, so that an abandoned choice never looks like an error or a switch.
4. As a user, I want Beadsmith to accept only directories that belong to initialized Beadwork Git repositories, so that a selected Workspace is usable for Issue Explorer operations.
5. As a user who chooses a subdirectory within a valid repository, I want Beadsmith to use the repository root rather than store the subdirectory as another Workspace, so that the catalog has one meaningful identity for that repository.
6. As a user, I want invalid or non-Beadwork directories to show clear inline selector feedback, so that I know why no Workspace changed.
7. As a user who selects a valid Workspace, I want to see its complete All Issues, Ready, and Blocked views before it is called Current, so that the displayed name and Issue data always agree.
8. As a user with a Workspace that contains no Issues, I want it to become Current and show the normal true-empty Issue Explorer state, so that an empty repository is not mistaken for a failed selection.
9. As a returning user, I want Beadsmith to restore my last successfully loaded Current Workspace after restart, so that I can continue where I left off.
10. As a returning user whose remembered Workspace no longer exists or cannot load, I want it to remain available for retry or removal without being presented as Current, so that I retain control without seeing a false active state.
11. As a user with several Workspaces, I want the selector to list remembered Workspaces in most-recently-used order with a basename and full path, so that I can quickly distinguish and return to the right repository.
12. As a user, I want a Workspace to move to the top of the catalog only after it has successfully loaded as Current, so that failed attempts do not corrupt my recency order.
13. As a user, I want to remove a remembered Workspace without confirmation, so that I can clean Beadsmith local memory without affecting the repository or Beadwork data.
14. As a user who removes the Current Workspace, I want Beadsmith to show the empty chooser instead of silently selecting another repository, so that the next Current Workspace is always my deliberate choice.
15. As a multi-workspace user, I want to switch from Workspace A to Workspace B without relaunching, so that I can work across repositories in one desktop session.
16. As a user switching Workspaces, I want Workspace A and its Issue Explorer snapshot to remain visible while Workspace B is pending, so that I never briefly read stale A data under B identity.
17. As a user whose candidate Workspace fails to load after validation, I want the prior Current Workspace to remain usable and a dismissible banner to offer retry, so that a transient problem does not interrupt my work.
18. As a user whose Workspace persistence fails, I want Beadsmith to keep the prior Current Workspace rather than claim an undurable switch, so that restart behavior matches what the app showed me.
19. As a user who quickly chooses multiple Workspaces, I want only my most recent selection to take effect, so that an older slow operation cannot overwrite my latest intent.
20. As a user, I want an unavailable remembered Workspace to remain in the catalog until I remove it, so that a temporarily disconnected or restored path is not silently forgotten.
21. As a developer using Beadsmith, I want Workspace changes in the real desktop binary to be covered by automated tests, so that native permissions, typed RPC, persistence, subprocess execution, and rendered UI compose correctly.
22. As a maintainer, I want the application to stop relying on process cwd and the startup `--workspace` override, so that Workspace ownership is explicit, testable, and safe under concurrent operations.

## Implementation Decisions

- **Domain model:** A Workspace is the root of a local Git repository initialized for Beadwork. The Workspace Catalog is a locally persisted MRU collection of remembered Workspaces. A Pending Workspace is a validated candidate currently loading and is never Current. An Unavailable Workspace is a remembered path that is missing or unreadable and remains retryable until explicit removal.

- **Backend ownership:** The backend owns the Workspace Catalog, Pending Workspace, Current Workspace, validation, persistence, and workspace-selection generations. The frontend may provide a candidate path only to a workspace-management operation. Issue Explorer loading operations remain path-free and use the backend-held Current Workspace.

- **Explicit command scope:** Every `bw` subprocess receives an explicit working directory. The command-runner seam accepts that directory, and the Issue Explorer adapter receives it for All Issues, Ready, and Blocked. Process cwd is removed from production Workspace resolution; changing the process cwd at runtime is not permitted.

- **Validation and identity:** Candidate validation runs Beadwork configuration inspection in the candidate directory and independently discovers the Git repository root. Success from a descendant path is normalized to that root before catalog identity, loading, or persistence. Configuration output is not used as a repository identity. Ordinary validated root paths define v1 identity; special symlink canonicalization and alias deduplication are intentionally omitted.

- **Persistence:** The Workspace Catalog uses one backend-owned, versioned persistent store with explicit save operations. The catalog is capped at 100 entries. Exact known paths do not create duplicates. A validated candidate is retained as known after validation succeeds, even if loading later fails. A Workspace is promoted in MRU order only after a successful complete switch. Current Workspace and the MRU update save together as the final switch commit.

- **Startup restoration:** On launch, the remembered last Current Workspace is restored only through the same validation-and-load transaction used for a user selection. A failed restore leaves no Current Workspace. A missing path remains Unavailable; a valid but unloadable path remains a retryable known Workspace.

- **Atomic switch transaction:** A workspace switch gets a monotonically increasing generation. The backend marks the target Pending, validates it, retains it in the known catalog, loads all three Issue Explorer views, saves Current Workspace plus MRU state, then publishes the complete new snapshot. After every asynchronous boundary, a stale generation is discarded. Only the latest request may persist or publish.

- **Failure behavior:** Invalid paths use inline selector feedback and change no current or persisted state. A load or persistence failure after validation retains the candidate for retry. When an old Current Workspace exists, its name and complete Issue Explorer snapshot remain visible and a dismissible retry banner explains the failure. When no Current Workspace exists, the app remains at the empty chooser rather than presenting a failed candidate as Current.

- **Selector design:** The existing sidebar Current Workspace area becomes the compact inline selector. It shows the active Workspace basename with full path as supporting text, lists remembered Workspaces nearby, exposes the native Choose folder action, distinguishes Unavailable Workspaces, and provides local-only removal. Custom Workspace renaming is not included.

- **Tauri integration:** Beadsmith adds the native dialog capability with the narrow permission needed to open a single directory picker. Persistent store access remains backend-only. The picker result is asynchronous; cancellation is a no-op. No frontend filesystem permission or frontend store access is needed merely to pass a selected path to the backend.

- **Migration:** The process-cwd startup override and its associated command-line argument are removed once the explicit Workspace path is active. Existing desktop tests start with no selected Workspace and seed fixtures through the same typed workspace-switch transport used by the product. Existing Issue List and Issue Detail behavior remains unchanged after a Workspace has successfully loaded.

## Testing Decisions

- A good test observes user-visible or contract-visible behavior rather than private implementation steps. It should prove that the correct Workspace data is shown, that failed switches preserve the previous state, and that stale operations cannot publish, rather than asserting internal call counts or implementation layout.

- The primary backend seam is the Workspace switch transaction service, which owns validation, state transitions, generations, and the persistence commit boundary. The existing command-runner seam remains the highest appropriate subprocess seam for proving explicit working-directory use without spawning a real `bw` in unit tests.

- Rust unit tests cover candidate validation and root normalization, catalog cap and MRU rules, unavailable retention, local-only removal, startup restoration, switch success, validation failure, load failure, persistence failure, retry, and stale generations. Adapter and command-runner tests prove every Beadwork view command receives the requested working directory and production loading does not resolve process cwd.

- Typed RPC integration tests cover workspace state, switch/retry/remove operations, generated bindings, and the fact that Issue Explorer loading carries no caller-supplied path. Frontend integration tests cover the empty chooser, picker wiring and cancellation, inline validation feedback, pending-state rendering, prior-snapshot preservation, banners, retry, catalog presentation, unavailable entries, and removal.

- The real desktop WebDriver suite is required for acceptance. It launches the built binary with no startup workspace override, uses the normal typed switch transport to select disposable Beadwork repositories, proves populated and true-empty Workspaces, proves atomic A-to-B switching, and proves invalid-target preservation. Direct typed transport is appropriate in WebDriver because operating-system dialogs are not a reliable WebDriver surface; frontend integration tests cover the picker-control wiring.

- Existing Issue Explorer unit, component, and WebDriver fixtures are prior art for the full data path from Beadwork CLI adapter through typed RPC and React. Existing command-runner fakes are prior art for subprocess and adapter tests. The test suite must continue to use real `bw` commands when constructing disposable desktop fixtures.

## Out of Scope

- Automatic issue refresh while a Workspace remains selected. A switch performs an immediate full load, but ongoing polling, focus refresh, and ref watching belong to the separate automatic-refresh effort.
- Custom names or user-editable aliases for Workspaces.
- Treating a selected Beadwork subdirectory as a distinct Workspace. It is normalized to the repository root instead.
- Special handling or deduplication of symlink aliases beyond ordinary path handling.
- Initializing Beadwork in an arbitrary selected directory. The selector validates existing initialized Workspaces only.
- A user-facing last-refreshed indicator, configurable workspace catalog size, broad visual regression testing, or a new manual refresh control.

## Further Notes

The implementation work is sequenced as an explicit path-scoped Workspace foundation, first-time selection, catalog restoration and management, atomic switching, and final real-desktop verification. The order avoids parallel changes inventing incompatible persistence, validation, or generation rules.

The accepted architecture remains explicit backend path threading and persisted backend state. The key product invariant is simple: the visible Current Workspace and Issue Explorer snapshot are one unit, and neither changes until the entire candidate transaction and durability commit succeed.

comments.text:
## Delivery graph and planning rationale

This epic intentionally begins with a narrow expand-stage seam because explicit cwd threading, validation, persistence, and typed workspace state are shared mechanics. It then delivers vertical user outcomes in order: first selection, remembered catalog, and safe multi-workspace switching. The final task is a real-desktop integration gate; it does not replace the per-slice test obligations.

```mermaid
graph LR
  A[Establish path-scoped Workspace foundation] --> B[Choose and load an initial Current Workspace]
  B --> C[Restore and manage the Workspace Catalog]
  B --> D[Switch Workspaces atomically and recover from failures]
  C --> D
  B --> E[Prove and document Workspace management end to end]
  C --> E
  D --> E
```

The graph is deliberately mostly sequential: each later behavior relies on the same backend-owned Workspace state and makes stronger promises about it. This avoids parallel work inventing incompatible catalog, persistence, or generation semantics.

title: Implement Workspace management
